chunk_text:
Design by
Duyen Tran

Photos by
Nicholas Johnson &
Jesse Wang Photography
The Sustainable Solutions Lab (SSL) at UMass Boston is
a collaborative research and action institute focused on
keeping historically excluded people and communities safe
and healthy as our climate changes. We are a groundbreaking, cross-disciplinary partnership of six
schools and four institutes across the University:
• Collaborative of Asian American, Native American,
Latino, and African American Institutes (CANALA)
• College of Education and Human Development
• College of Liberal Arts
• College of Management
• Robert and Donna Manning College of Nursing and
Health Sciences
• John W.

chunk_text:
We’re working to keep historically excluded
people and communities safe and healthy as
our climate changes. SSL’s direction and work is guided by several
committees and groups. We have dozens of
Faculty Affiliates from the colleges, schools, and
institutes that make up SSL. They are
represented by a Core Team that helps guide our
decision-making and strategy. We also have an
Oversight Committee made up of Deans and
Directors who provide internal guidance and a
Board of Directors, composed of external
advisers.

chunk_text:
As climate disasters mount and inequality grows, it is easy to feel fearful
about the future. Thankfully, we have been heartened by the ways our
work is growing and the impact we are having. We had our most productive year of research to date. From exploring
the intersections between the housing crisis and the climate crisis, to
how equity is incorporated into the State of Massachusetts’ Municipal
Vulnerability Preparedness program, to shining a light on how the Greater
Boston climate adaptation field is operating, our applied research is
informing decision makers’ efforts to ground their work in equity. Over the past year, UMass Boston has gone through a major strategic
planning process.

chunk_text:
From exploring
the intersections between the housing crisis and the climate crisis, to
how equity is incorporated into the State of Massachusetts’ Municipal
Vulnerability Preparedness program, to shining a light on how the Greater
Boston climate adaptation field is operating, our applied research is
informing decision makers’ efforts to ground their work in equity. Over the past year, UMass Boston has gone through a major strategic
planning process. One of the outcomes of this process is a university-wide
Grand Scholarly Challenge of “Climate Equity and Urban Coastal Areas.”
This is building on the foundation our faculty, students, and staff have
developed over the last few years.

chunk_text:
Over the past year, UMass Boston has gone through a major strategic
planning process. One of the outcomes of this process is a university-wide
Grand Scholarly Challenge of “Climate Equity and Urban Coastal Areas.”
This is building on the foundation our faculty, students, and staff have
developed over the last few years. We are excited to work with colleagues
to weave climate justice throughout the university—from coursework and
experiential learning, to research and operations. We know that together
we can prepare our students to be leaders in a climate-disrupted future. Another achievement this year is the launch of the Northeast Climate
Justice Research Collaborative this past spring.

chunk_text:
We know that together
we can prepare our students to be leaders in a climate-disrupted future. Another achievement this year is the launch of the Northeast Climate
Justice Research Collaborative this past spring. Building upon the
successful creation of a community of climate justice research at UMass
Boston, this collaborative expands our reach regionally and allows
us to connect with many more scholars. From new transdisciplinary
partnerships, to training resources, to supportive relationships, we look
forward to watching the seeds we are planting bloom. Thank you for being part of the SSL community.

chunk_text:
SSL is working to broaden the definition
of what “counts” as climate change research to spur novel
approaches and research breakthroughs. Through these efforts, SSL can ensure that those who are best
positioned to bring these novel perspectives (or perspectives that
have historically been sidelined) can engage and influence the climate
adaptation field. We are committed to cultivating a transdisciplinary
community of practice to support diverse faculty's research and their
development as scholars, as they are essential to generating the
solutions and policies needed for equitable adaptation. En Español
Selected Research Releases by Affiliated Faculty and Students

chunk_text:
“There are many missing connections and missed
opportunities for collaboration. The most notable
collaboration gap is between the institutional and grassroots groups.
embedding_text:
“There are many missing connections and missed
opportunities for collaboration. The most notable
collaboration gap is between the institutional and grassroots groups.

chunk_text:
The most notable
collaboration gap is between the institutional and grassroots groups. More work is needed to better align and
integrate climate adaptation efforts focused on physical
resilience, driven primarily by private and public sector
groups, with social resilience efforts driven by non-profit,
grassroots organizations. ...By exploring the implications
of these collaborative connections, stakeholders can
examine relative gaps and opportunities for true climate
resilience in our region.”
Participants left the release event excited and curious
about how understanding current power dynamics can
help move the field toward equity.

integrate climate adaptation efforts focused on physical
resilience, driven primarily by private and public sector
groups, with social resilience efforts driven by non-profit,
grassroots organizations. ...By exploring the implications
of these collaborative connections, stakeholders can
examine relative gaps and opportunities for true climate
resilience in our region.”
Participants left the release event excited and curious
about how understanding current power dynamics can
help move the field toward equity. Since then, SSL has
connected people for over 40 conversations to begin
intentionally weaving connections across resilience types.

chunk_text:
•
Novel Research in Climate Justice: Including Transient Populations in
Climate Resiliency
Research Team: Ellen Douglas, Johnna Flahive, Evan Stewart
»
This research project will characterize the status and vulnerability of transient
populations (homeless citizens and seasonal international visa workers) within the
context of climate-related risks, focusing on Boston and Cape Cod, Massachusetts. •
Voices that Matter Focus Group Report
Research Team: Lorena Estrada-Martinez, Katsysris Rivera-Kientz, Paul Watanabe
with support from Isa Whalen and Chidimma Ozor Commer
»
This report is a follow on to the Views that Matter project released in September of
2020.

This report explores the findings from focus group conversations conducted
with different racial groups about how climate change is impacting their lives. •
Developing a Shared Understanding of our History: A Land-Use-Based View
of How Climate Change Affects People and Neighborhoods in Boston
Research Team: Nicholas H. Johnson, Antonio Raciti
»
This project is being conducted as part of a partnership with the Boston Green Ribbon
Commission and King Boston with the goal to develop a shared understanding of
Boston’s history and patterns of development that have led to current inequities that
are exacerbated by climate change.

chunk_text:
•
Climate Justice, Traditional Ecological Knowledge, and Relationality among
Indigenous Peoples of New England and Quintana Roo, Mexico
Research Team: Camille Curtis Martinez, Jose Martinez-Reyes, C. Eduardo
Siqueira, Cedric Woods
»
This is an interdisciplinary study of traditional ecological knowledge and climate
change adaptations among the Indigenous peoples of New England and Quintana Roo,
Mexico. This project examines how Indigenous peoples have been among the first to
develop knowledge and adaptations based on their relations and observations about
the changes in the climate and its effect on their environment. View Research
embedding_text:
•
Climate Justice, Traditional Ecological Knowledge, and Relationality among
Indigenous Peoples of New England and Quintana Roo, Mexico
Research Team: Camille Curtis Martinez, Jose Martinez-Reyes, C. Eduardo
Siqueira, Cedric Woods
»
This is an interdisciplinary study of traditional ecological knowledge and climate
change adaptations among the Indigenous peoples of New England and Quintana Roo,
Mexico. This project examines how Indigenous peoples have been among the first to
develop knowledge and adaptations based on their relations and observations about
the changes in the climate and its effect on their environment. View Research

chunk_text:
SSL students are the backbone of our research projects. They pose novel questions and push projects forward. These students worked on a wide variety of SSL research
projects over the past year. Caitlin Connelly published a comprehensive review of climate
change effects on older adults focusing on key vulnerability
factors including chronic conditions and care dependency, social
isolation, socioeconomic disadvantage, and substandard housing.

chunk_text:
These students worked on a wide variety of SSL research
projects over the past year. Caitlin Connelly published a comprehensive review of climate
change effects on older adults focusing on key vulnerability
factors including chronic conditions and care dependency, social
isolation, socioeconomic disadvantage, and substandard housing. Patricio Belloy completed an assessment of the resilience planning
in East Boston through a climate justice lens, a case study looking at
how climate research informs public policy discussions, and a project
exploring how the climate crisis will impact the housing crisis.

Patricio Belloy completed an assessment of the resilience planning
in East Boston through a climate justice lens, a case study looking at
how climate research informs public policy discussions, and a project
exploring how the climate crisis will impact the housing crisis. Grace Derboghossian is working on a study examining
the association between living in an environmental justice
community in Boston and Suffolk County and its effect on
birth outcomes. Margaret Gatonye’s research
focuses on women’s marginalization
in Kenyan marine and freshwater
fisheries and how this is affected by
climate change.

exploring Traditional Ecological Knowledge and climate
change in the coming year. Katsyris Rivera-Kientz is working on projects
about equitable climate adaptation, public perceptions on climate change, and Puerto Rico's
post-disaster events.

chunk_text:
UMass Boston faculty and students are the foundation
of everything SSL does. We provide seed funding,
professional development, promotional support, and
climate justice guidance to scholars at various stages
of their academic journey—from graduate students to
tenured professors—but with a special emphasis on
junior faculty. This year, building on the work we have
done at UMass Boston, we launched the Northeast
Climate Justice Research Collaborative to expand our
reach. We are excited to continue to support students
and scholars in their efforts to advance climate justice.

racial justice in a way I never would have previously. The Sustainable Solutions Lab is transforming how
climate change researchers connect with each other
and the public by convening an interdisciplinary
group of scholars to inform climate change policies at
the state, local, and national level. By pairing rigorous
scientific research with an evidence-based approach
to policy engagement, its efforts promise to shape
public policy in the years to come.

In Spring of 2022, SSL launched the Northeast Climate Justice
Research Collaborative (The Collaborative). This network
brings together researchers across the Northeast who actively
work (or wish to expand their work) to advance concrete policy
and planning practices that ensure that historically excluded
communities are safe and healthy despite climate change.

chunk_text:
“
In Spring of 2022, SSL launched the Northeast Climate Justice
Research Collaborative (The Collaborative). This network
brings together researchers across the Northeast who actively
work (or wish to expand their work) to advance concrete policy
and planning practices that ensure that historically excluded
communities are safe and healthy despite climate change. Through networking, dialogue, and sharing research and
resources, we support scholars to maximize the benefits
of their research for the communities they are working with
and in. To date The Collaborative includes almost 100
researchers from almost 40 institutions in the Northeast.

In partnership with the Scholars Strategy
Network, we hosted a series of three training
sessions for members of The Collaborative. These workshops focused on how to share
research findings with policymakers and
decision-makers effectively.

Lacee A. Satcher
Assistant Professor of Sociology and Environmental Studies,
Boston College
Dr. Satcher’s research will focus on perceptions, knowledge, and
attitudes regarding climate change and urban greening among older
Black residents in Boston, MA and Brockton, MA – an often-overshadowed population in the research and public discourse on climate
change. The research will also examine the health impacts of climate
change on these individuals and their communities. Nurcan Atalan-Helicke
Associate Professor Environmental Studies & Sciences Program,
Skidmore College
Dr.

chunk_text:
Nurcan Atalan-Helicke
Associate Professor Environmental Studies & Sciences Program,
Skidmore College
Dr. Atalan-Helicke's project aims to examine how small farmers and
historically excluded communities (indigenous, BIPOC) in upstate New
York are responding to the changing climate through seed protection,
revival, and seed production strategies in their agricultural fields. The project involves semi-structured interviews with small farmers
participating in farmers' markets in upstate New York (Capital District
region), representatives of food sovereignty, and food justice organizations working on establishing seed gardens with locally adopted or
culturally important seeds.

chunk_text:
Selected Media Appearances
Climate Adaptation Forum

The Climate Adaptation Forum is a quarterly series of half-day events organized
by the Environmental Business Council and the Sustainable Solutions Lab at
the . These forums provide cutting-edge
thought leadership on adaptation to climate change for environmental and
energy professionals, policymakers, municipal officials, NGOs, and practitioners. Starting in 2017, these forums have been addressing a broad array of topics
through local, national, and global speakers representing forward-thinking
institutions, global firms, academia, government, and more.
embedding_text:
Selected Media Appearances
Climate Adaptation Forum

The Climate Adaptation Forum is a quarterly series of half-day events organized
by the Environmental Business Council and the Sustainable Solutions Lab at
the . These forums provide cutting-edge
thought leadership on adaptation to climate change for environmental and
energy professionals, policymakers, municipal officials, NGOs, and practitioners. Starting in 2017, these forums have been addressing a broad array of topics
through local, national, and global speakers representing forward-thinking
institutions, global firms, academia, government, and more.

chunk_text:
These forums provide cutting-edge
thought leadership on adaptation to climate change for environmental and
energy professionals, policymakers, municipal officials, NGOs, and practitioners. Starting in 2017, these forums have been addressing a broad array of topics
through local, national, and global speakers representing forward-thinking
institutions, global firms, academia, government, and more. They provide a
regular, ongoing opportunity for adaptation professionals from various fields
in the Boston area to meet and cross-pollinate ideas and share solutions to
common problems.

FEB 22, 2022 | WBUR | By Rebecca Herst
View More Events
SSL Director, Rebecca Herst, was awarded the
Peter McClure Award by the UMass Boston College
of Management. The award is a recognition of her
continuing success in promoting research and
developing programs that foster collaboration in all
forms of diversity for the betterment of the College, the
workforce, and society.
embedding_text:

FEB 22, 2022 | WBUR | By Rebecca Herst
View More Events
SSL Director, Rebecca Herst, was awarded the
Peter McClure Award by the UMass Boston College
of Management. The award is a recognition of her
continuing success in promoting research and
developing programs that foster collaboration in all
forms of diversity for the betterment of the College, the
workforce, and society.

chunk_text:
The award is a recognition of her
continuing success in promoting research and
developing programs that foster collaboration in all
forms of diversity for the betterment of the College, the
workforce, and society. As stated in the award letter:

"Under your leadership, the Sustainable Solutions
Lab has developed a reputation as a respected
source of expertise on both climate and social
equity and for fostering relationships across fields
to catalyze change."
From June 4-10, SSL faculty and doctoral students, in
collaboration with the Alliance for Business Leadership,
led a study tour to Denmark to learn about green
transition and climate adaptation.
embedding_text:

continuing success in promoting research and

forms of diversity for the betterment of the College, the

From June 4-10, SSL faculty and doctoral students, in

transition and climate adaptation.

chunk_text:
As stated in the award letter:

"Under your leadership, the Sustainable Solutions
Lab has developed a reputation as a respected
source of expertise on both climate and social
equity and for fostering relationships across fields
to catalyze change."
From June 4-10, SSL faculty and doctoral students, in
collaboration with the Alliance for Business Leadership,
led a study tour to Denmark to learn about green
transition and climate adaptation. Funded by The Barr
Foundation, the group toured Copenhagen and other
areas to learn about programs, financing, and projects all
intended to help Denmark transition to a non-fossil fuelbased economy.

Lab has developed a reputation as a respected
source of expertise on both climate and social
equity and for fostering relationships across fields
to catalyze change."
From June 4-10, SSL faculty and doctoral students, in
collaboration with the Alliance for Business Leadership,
led a study tour to Denmark to learn about green
transition and climate adaptation. Funded by The Barr
Foundation, the group toured Copenhagen and other
areas to learn about programs, financing, and projects all
intended to help Denmark transition to a non-fossil fuelbased economy.

intended to help Denmark transition to a non-fossil fuelbased economy. The group included SSL faculty Michael
Johnson, Amit Patel, and Robert Chen, and SSL doctoral
students Jessica Lillquist, Margaret Gatonye, Patricio
Belloy, and Kelsey Edmond—representing subject areas
such as Environmental Science, Global Governance
and Human Security, and Public Policy.

chunk_text:
We feel grateful to be a part of a community of researchers and practitioners
pushing to make a more just and resilient world for all. Thank you for your
continued commitment to this work and for your partnership. Thank you to our partners.
